Focus on final validation across the core user flow while finishing implementation details, tightening tests, improving docs, and polishing the codebase for handoff."},{"dayIndex":4,"type":"handoff","title":"Handoff + Demo","description":"Prepare a concise handoff demo that walks through the implemented solution, key decisions, tradeoffs, outcomes, and remaining risks for reviewers."},{"dayIndex":5,"type":"reflection","title":"Reflection Essay","description":"Write a markdown reflection essay covering your experience, challenges, decisions, tradeoffs, communication, and what you would do next."}],"projectBriefMd":"# Fresh End-To-End Proof After Live Bundle/Runtime Repairs

## Context

the team is trying to improve Fresh end-to-end proof after live bundle/runtime repairs.
The work should feel like a real backend engineer trial: the candidate starts from an empty repository and is expected to build the system from scratch.
Domain: B2B developer tooling; Product area: workflow automation platform

## Problem

The candidate must design and build a Fresh end-to-end proof after live bundle/runtime repairs that solves a concrete business problem with enough detail to grade the work consistently.

## Users

- Primary users: the operational team that depends on the new workflow or service.
- Secondary users: the Talent Partner reviewing the Evidence Trail and demo.
- Preferred language/framework context: Python, FastAPI, pytest. Treat this as a constraint to respect, not a command to follow blindly.

## Functional Requirements

- Build a production-shaped system, feature, service, or tool from scratch.
- Support the core user journey and the main success path end to end.
- Include the minimum data model, APIs, and persistence needed for the workflow.
- Make the implementation testable and understandable without prescribing the exact file layout.

## Non-Functional Requirements

- Keep the scope achievable in two focused implementation days.
- Choose an architecture that is reliable, secure enough for the trial, and easy to explain.
- Avoid unnecessary framework lock-in beyond the preferred language/framework context.
- Surface errors clearly and make the system observable enough for review.

## Out of Scope

- Do not build unrelated features, admin tooling, or extra user journeys.
- Do not prescribe exact file structures or endpoint names.
- Do not assume implementation files beyond the empty-workspace infrastructure.

## What "Done" Looks Like

- The requested workflow is implemented and behaves consistently in the candidate repository.
- Tests cover the meaningful success path and the important failure cases.
- The repository is easy to review, hand off, and demo within the Trial.

## Suggested Daily Cadence
- Day 1 (Design Doc): define the architecture, risks, tradeoffs, and validation plan.
- Day 2 (Implementation Kickoff): scaffold the system and land the first end-to-end slice.
- Day 3 (Implementation Wrap-Up): finish the core path, tighten tests, and harden edges.
- Day 4 (Handoff + Demo): walk through what was built, what was verified, and what remains open.
- Day 5 (Reflection): explain the decisions, mistakes, and lessons from the Trial.","rubricJson":{"summary":"Evaluate Backend Engineer performance across design, implementation, Handoff + Demo, and ai.noticeText: We use AI to help evaluate submitted work artifacts, coding outputs, and communication signals across the trial. Human reviewers oversee AI-generated findings and final hiring decisions are made by people.

scenario.storylineMd:
# Backend Engineer Scenario v1

You are joining a product team with an empty repository and a blank slate.
The team needs a from-scratch build for an incident-prone integration that needs stronger reliability boundaries.
The trial is intended to surface product judgment, build quality, and communication under a two-day implementation window with tight operational observability from day one.
Treat the scenario as production hiring work, not an interview toy.
The product area is framed around a realistic company need, and the candidate is free to choose a reasonable implementation approach.

taskPromptsJson.title: Architecture Plan
taskPromptsJson.description: Draft an implementation plan that defines service boundaries, key data flows, API contracts, and risk controls. Include concrete tradeoffs and how you will validate correctness with tests and reviewable artifacts.

taskPromptsJson.title: Feature Implementation
taskPromptsJson.description: Implement the primary backend slice in code with tests. Prioritize correctness under realistic edge cases and keep the solution aligned with the brief.

taskPromptsJson.title: Implementation Wrap-Up
taskPromptsJson.description: Continue the implementation wrap-up in the same repository used on Day 2. Focus on final validation across the core user flow while finishing implementation details, tightening tests, improving docs, and polishing the codebase for handoff.

taskPromptsJson.title: Handoff + Demo
taskPromptsJson.description: Prepare a concise handoff demo that walks through the implemented solution, key decisions, tradeoffs, outcomes, and remaining risks for reviewers.

taskPromptsJson.title: Reflection Essay
taskPromptsJson.description: Write a markdown reflection essay covering your experience, challenges, decisions, tradeoffs, communication, and what you would do next.

scenario.projectBriefMd:
# Fresh End-To-End Proof After Live Bundle/Runtime Repairs

## Context

the team is trying to improve Fresh end-to-end proof after live bundle/runtime repairs.
The work should feel like a real backend engineer trial: the candidate starts from an empty repository and is expected to build the system from scratch.
Domain: B2B developer tooling; Product area: workflow automation platform

## Problem

The candidate must design and build a Fresh end-to-end proof after live bundle/runtime repairs that solves a concrete business problem with enough detail to grade the work consistently.

## Users

- Primary users: the operational team that depends on the new workflow or service.
- Secondary users: the Talent Partner reviewing the Evidence Trail and demo.
- Preferred language/framework context: Python, FastAPI, pytest. Treat this as a constraint to respect, not a command to follow blindly.

## Functional Requirements

- Build a production-shaped system, feature, service, or tool from scratch.
- Support the core user journey and the main success path end to end.
- Include the minimum data model, APIs, and persistence needed for the workflow.
- Make the implementation testable and understandable without prescribing the exact file layout.

## Non-Functional Requirements

- Keep the scope achievable in two focused implementation days.
- Choose an architecture that is reliable, secure enough for the trial, and easy to explain.
- Avoid unnecessary framework lock-in beyond the preferred language/framework context.
- Surface errors clearly and make the system observable enough for review.

## Out of Scope

- Do not build unrelated features, admin tooling, or extra user journeys.
- Do not prescribe exact file structures or endpoint names.
- Do not assume implementation files beyond the empty-workspace infrastructure.

## What "Done" Looks Like

- The requested workflow is implemented and behaves consistently in the candidate repository.
- Tests cover the meaningful success path and the important failure cases.
- The repository is easy to review, hand off, and demo within the Trial.

## Suggested Daily Cadence
- Day 1 (Design Doc): define the architecture, risks, tradeoffs, and validation plan.
- Day 2 (Implementation Kickoff): scaffold the system and land the first end-to-end slice.
- Day 3 (Implementation Wrap-Up): finish the core path, tighten tests, and harden edges.
- Day 4 (Handoff + Demo): walk through what was built, what was verified, and what remains open.
- Day 5 (Reflection): explain the decisions, mistakes, and lessons from the Trial.

rubricJson.summary: Evaluate Backend Engineer performance across design, implementation, Handoff + Demo, and reflection in a from-scratch build.

dimensions.name: Architecture & Design
dimensions.description: Shows a workable architecture, boundaries, and data flow for the Trial.

dimensions.name: Problem Understanding
dimensions.description: Reframes the business need accurately and keeps the brief grounded in real stakes.

dimensions.name: Functional Requirements
dimensions.description: Captures the core product behaviors that must actually work.

dimensions.name: Non-Functional Requirements
dimensions.description: States reliability, security, and performance expectations without over-specifying the stack.

dimensions.name: Scope Realism
dimensions.description: Keeps the work achievable in the 2-day implementation window.

dimensions.name: Code Quality
dimensions.description: Sets expectations for readable, maintainable, and well-structured code.

dimensions.name: Testing
dimensions.description: Requires meaningful verification of the important paths and failure cases.

dimensions.name: Communication
dimensions.description: Requires clear handoff language that a Talent Partner can follow.
